Were You Asked This?
Design a Ticket Booking System

Design a ticket booking system like Ticketmaster that allows users to search for events, book tickets (both reserved seating and general admission), and handle high-demand scenarios with virtual queues and ticket locking mechanisms.

Asked at:

Meta

Google

Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

A ticket booking system like Ticketmaster lets people discover events, pick specific seats or general admission quantities, place a short hold, pay, and receive digital tickets. Think concerts, theater, sports, movies, or even airline seats—users expect live availability, fair access during surges, and a smooth checkout.

Interviewers ask this because it forces you to reason about extreme contention on a tiny inventory set, short-lived reservations with expiration, correctness under retries, and fairness controls (waiting rooms). It also touches payment orchestration, idempotency, and real-time updates—skills that map to many high-stakes, stateful product flows beyond ticketing.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to discover and search for events by keyword, date, and location.

Users should be able to view ticket availability, including seat maps for reserved seating and quantities for general admission.

Users should be able to place a short reservation hold on selected tickets that automatically expires.

Users should be able to complete purchase, including payment, and receive a confirmed booking with tickets.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you prevent overselling and guarantee correctness under extreme contention for both reserved seating and general admission inventory?

How do you implement short-lived reservations (e.g., 5 minutes) without long-running database locks or interactive transactions?

How would you design a virtual waiting room to provide fairness and protect the system during a flash sale or surge?

How do you ensure idempotency and correctness across the payment and booking flow under retries, timeouts, and webhook replays?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Dealing with Contention

Ticket drops concentrate massive write contention on a tiny set of seats or a single GA counter. You need atomic updates, short critical sections, and fairness controls to avoid oversells and meltdown.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

Checkout spans reservation, payment authorization/capture, and ticket issuance. Modeling this as a clear state machine or saga with compensations and idempotency is essential for correctness.

Read More
Real-time Updates

Users expect live seat map changes, queue position updates, and hold countdowns. Push-based updates (WebSockets/SSE) reduce wasteful polling and improve UX during high demand.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

Redis provides low-latency, atomic operations for hot-path inventory: GA counters via Lua, distributed locks with TTL for per-seat holds, rate limiting for admission, and ephemeral state like waiting-room positions.

Read More
PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL delivers strong transactional guarantees for seat states, reservations, and bookings. Optimistic concurrency control, unique constraints, and short transactions help ensure no double-sell.

Read More
Kafka

Kafka supports durable event-driven workflows for booking confirmations, email delivery, auditing, and reconciliation. Paired with an outbox, it ensures reliable, exactly-once semantics at the system boundary.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Ticketmaster

It is the canonical version of this problem: reserved seating vs GA, extreme contention, reservation expiry, virtual waiting rooms, and payment finalization.

Read
Practice
Online Auction

Both have intense competition for scarce items, require strict correctness under concurrent writes, and benefit from fairness mechanisms and idempotent operations.

Read
Practice
Payment System

Booking flows rely on payment authorization/capture, idempotency keys, webhook handling, and state-machine design that mirrors a Stripe-like integration.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Holding long database locks or interactive transactions for the entire reservation window

This ties up scarce resources, risks deadlocks, and collapses under load. A crash could wedge inventory indefinitely and the system won’t scale during surges.

No admission control or waiting room during high-demand events

Letting everyone hit the booking endpoints creates cascading failures, timeouts, and an unfair race. A queue protects downstream services and improves fairness.

Missing idempotency and explicit state transitions across payment and booking

Without idempotency keys, an outbox/inbox, and clear state checks, retries and out-of-order webhooks can cause double charges, double bookings, or orphaned holds.
